title: Khoresh bademjan
subtitle: Persian aubergine and lamb stew with saffron and tomato
excerpt: A Persian khoresh (stew) made with fried aubergine, lamb, saffron, and tomatoes, slow-cooked until the aubergine has melted into a silky, intensely savoury sauce. The aubergines are fried first to deepen their flavour and prevent them from disintegrating into the sauce; they should hold their shape while being tender enough to crush with a spoon.
sourceNotes: Khoresh bademjan (Persian for 'aubergine stew') is one of the classic khoresh of Persian cooking, documented in Margaret Shaida's 'The Legendary Cuisine of Persia' (1992) and Najmieh Batmanglij's 'Food of Life' (1986). Khoresh (stew) is the category of dishes served with Persian rice. The aubergine version is one of the most widely made in Iranian households.

content.text: The aubergines must be fried separately before joining the stew. They absorb oil readily, but this is correct, the oil carries the aubergine's flavour into the sauce. Use enough oil to get good colour on all sides. They go into the stew in the last 30 minutes so they soften further without collapsing entirely. Long cooking produces a more integrated sauce; adding them too early produces mush.

Saffron is not optional in this dish. It colours the sauce gold and adds a faint floral depth. The tomatoes and tomato paste thicken the stew and add the fruity acidity that balances the richness of the lamb and aubergine. Serve with Persian rice (tahdig) for the full effect.
Fry the aubergine and brown the lamb

Salt the {{aubergine}} pieces and leave for 20 minutes. Pat dry. Heat 3 tablespoons of the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large pan and fry the aubergine in batches until golden on all sides, about 5 minutes. Remove and set aside. In the same pan with the remaining oil, brown the {{lamb-shoulder}} in batches over high heat. Remove.

Reduce heat to medium. Cook the {{onion}} for 10 minutes. Add the {{turmeric}} and {{cinnamon-ground}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{tomato-puree}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}}, {{saffron}} water, and 200 ml water. Return the lamb, season with salt, bring to a simmer, cover, and cook for 50 minutes. Add the fried {{aubergine}} and {{lemon}} juice and cook uncovered for 30 minutes more until the sauce is thick and the lamb is tender.

items.fix: Aubergine goes in for the last 30 minutes only. Even then it should be watched. It will soften quickly in the hot sauce
items.cause: Added too early or cooked too long after adding
items.symptom: Aubergine has disintegrated into the sauce

items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 30 minutes to allow the sauce to reduce. The finished sauce should coat the back of a spoon
items.cause: Lid on too long or too much liquid added

items.fix: Salting and resting for 20 minutes draws out excess moisture and some of the bitterness. Pat thoroughly dry before frying
Steep saffron in hot water for at least 10 minutes before adding. The colour should be vivid orange-gold, not pale yellow
items.symptom: No saffron colour in the sauce

content.text: Khoresh bademjan is one of the most popular khoresh in Iran, made in homes year-round and served at family meals over white Persian rice. The khoresh (stew) is the counterpart to Persian rice in the meal structure, the rice absorbs the sauce, the meat provides substance, and the two together constitute the main course. Aubergine is a staple of Persian cooking, appearing in khoresh, in dips (kashk bademjan, mirza ghasemi), and in side dishes. The combination of saffron and aubergine is a classic Persian pairing, giving the stew its distinctive deep golden colour and fragrant depth.
